description: A three-sided on-demand marketplace matching diners (demand), restaurants/merchants (supply), and couriers (fulfillment). Levers are liquidity (matching demand to available couriers), unit economics (basket size, take rate, delivery cost), reliability (delivery time, cancellation), and retention (repeat ordering).

metrics.name: Average Delivery Time
metrics.definition: Average elapsed time from order placed to order delivered.
metrics.formula: AVG(delivered_at - placed_at) over delivered orders, expressed in minutes
metrics.grain: Order grain — exactly one placed and one delivered timestamp per order. Compute the duration per order FIRST, then average. Restrict to orders that actually reached delivered status; cancelled/undelivered orders have no delivered_at and must be excluded, not treated as zero.
anti_patterns: Joining orders to multiple courier-leg or status-event rows before averaging duplicates each order and biases the mean toward multi-leg orders — collapse to one row per order first.
AVG over all orders including cancelled ones (NULL delivered_at) either errors or, if coalesced to 0/now(), corrupts the mean.
Mixing units — subtracting epoch seconds but labeling the result minutes inflates the value 60x; pick a unit and convert once.
Using courier-accepted or picked-up time as the start instead of order placed understates the diner-experienced time.
metrics.sane_range: minutes, strictly positive; typically 20–60 min. Sub-5 or >180 usually signals a unit error or stuck/abandoned orders.

metrics.name: Orders per Active User
metrics.definition: Average number of orders placed per active user within a defined period.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT active_customer_id), 0) within the period
metrics.grain: Customer grain over a fixed period. 'Active' MUST be defined by the period (e.g. customers with ≥1 order in the month / week); the denominator is distinct active customers, not all-time registered users. Numerator and denominator must cover the SAME window.
anti_patterns: Dividing by all registered users (including dormant/never-ordered) collapses frequency toward zero — count only users active in the window.
Counting order line-items or basket rows instead of distinct orders fans out the numerator.
Mismatched windows (orders this month ÷ users active all-time) produces a meaningless ratio.
Joining orders to customers without DISTINCT can multiply customers that have multiple addresses/payment methods.
metrics.sane_range: ratio ≥ 1 within an active window (an active user has at least 1 order); typically 1–10 per month. A value < 1 means the denominator is wrong (non-active users included).

metrics.name: Average Order Value (AOV)
metrics.definition: Average gross merchandise value per order (typically food subtotal, before or after fees depending on definition — be consistent).
metrics.grain: Order grain. If value lives on basket/menu-item rows, SUM the line value and divide by COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) — never average line totals or item prices.
anti_patterns: AVG(order_items.line_total) computes average ITEM/LINE value, not order value.
After joining orders to basket items, COUNT(*) counts items not orders — dividing GMV by it understates AOV.
Joining orders to courier or restaurant rows before summing duplicates GMV (fan-out) and inflates the total; aggregate at order grain first.
Inconsistently including/excluding delivery fee, service fee, tip, and tax across queries makes AOV non-comparable.
metrics.sane_range: currency, strictly positive; typically low tens to ~100 per order. Negative or zero indicates refunds/voids leaking into GMV.

metrics.name: Cancellation Rate
metrics.definition: Share of orders that are cancelled, optionally split by who cancelled (customer / restaurant / courier / system).
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT cancelled_order_id) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT order_id), 0); split by cancelled_by for attribution
metrics.grain: Order grain. The DENOMINATOR is ALL orders placed (delivered + cancelled + in-progress), NOT just delivered orders. Numerator and denominator are the same order population. For per-party rates, partition the numerator by cancellation initiator while keeping the SAME all-orders denominator.
anti_patterns: Denominator = delivered orders only → mathematically excludes cancellations from the base and overstates the rate (or makes per-party rates not sum correctly).
Counting status-event rows (an order can log multiple status changes) instead of distinct cancelled orders inflates the numerator.
Summing customer + restaurant + courier rates computed against different denominators — they won't add up; use one shared denominator.
Joining to courier rows fans out orders and double-counts both numerator and denominator.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1; healthy marketplaces typically 0.02–0.10 overall. A rate > 1 is a join fan-out bug, not real data.

metrics.name: Courier Utilization (Deliveries per Online Hour)
metrics.definition: Average number of deliveries completed per hour a courier is online/available.
metrics.formula: SUM(deliveries_completed) / NULLIF(SUM(online_hours), 0)  per courier then aggregate
metrics.grain: Courier-period grain. Aggregate deliveries and online hours per courier (and time window) at the SAME grain before dividing; online time comes from shift/availability logs, deliveries from completed orders. A courier with zero online hours yields NULL (undefined), not zero.
anti_patterns: Joining couriers to their online-session rows AND to delivery rows in one query is a chasm join — it multiplies hours by deliveries; pre-aggregate each side per courier first.
Counting order rows assigned to a courier that were later reassigned/cancelled overstates completed deliveries — count only deliveries the courier actually completed.
Dividing total deliveries by total online hours across all couriers without aligning the same population/window hides idle couriers.
Treating couriers with NULL online hours as 0 utilization conflates 'never logged on' with 'unproductive'.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..~4 deliveries/hour typical; > ~6/hr is unusual and suggests double-counted deliveries or undercounted hours.

metrics.name: Repeat / Retention Rate (Cohort)
metrics.definition: Share of a first-order cohort that places another order in a later period.
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT retained_customer_id) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT cohort_customer_id), 0), where the cohort = customers whose FIRST order fell in period P and retained = those with ≥1 order in a later period
metrics.grain: Customer grain, cohorted by first-order period. Compute each customer's first-order date FIRST (MIN over their orders), assign to a cohort, then measure later-period activity. Numerator customers must be a subset of the cohort denominator.
anti_patterns: COUNT(orders) / COUNT(customers) is orders-per-customer, NOT retention.
Defining the cohort by order date instead of FIRST-order date double-assigns repeat customers to multiple cohorts.
Counting a customer as retained from any order including those in the cohort period itself — retention must be a LATER period than acquisition.
Windowing over an orders⋈customers join without pre-aggregating to one row per customer double-counts the denominator.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1; month-1 retention often 0.2–0.5, declining over later periods.

metrics.name: Take Rate
metrics.definition: Marketplace revenue (commissions + fees retained) as a share of gross merchandise value.
metrics.grain: Order grain. Commission and GMV MUST be summed at the SAME order grain before dividing — both numerator and denominator are per-order amounts. Never compute commission from one table and GMV from another at mismatched grains.
anti_patterns: Summing GMV from an orders⋈basket-items join (fanned out) while commission stays per-order, or vice-versa, makes the denominator and numerator inconsistent — collapse both to order grain first.
Including or excluding delivery/service fees inconsistently between commission and GMV (e.g. commission counts fees but GMV doesn't) distorts the rate.
Joining orders to courier/restaurant rows duplicates both amounts; the ratio may survive but per-segment breakdowns will be wrong.
AVG of per-order take-rate percentages is unweighted — a $5 order and a $200 order count equally; aggregate commission and GMV, then divide.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1, typically 0.10–0.30 (10–30%). A value > 1 means commission was summed against a fanned-out (too-small) or mismatched GMV.

metrics.name: Courier Acceptance Rate
metrics.definition: Share of delivery offers/dispatches that couriers accept.
metrics.grain: Offer/dispatch grain — one row per offer made to a courier. The denominator is all offers sent (accepted + declined + expired/ignored), the numerator is accepted offers. A single order can generate several offers (re-dispatches); count OFFERS, not orders.
anti_patterns: Using orders as the denominator instead of offers undercounts dispatch attempts (re-offers after declines) and overstates acceptance.
Treating expired/timed-out offers as neither accepted nor offered drops them from the denominator and inflates the rate — they belong in the base.
Counting offer-status events (an offer logs sent→accepted) instead of distinct offers double-counts the numerator.
Joining offers to courier rows without DISTINCT fans out when a courier has multiple profiles/vehicles.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1; typically 0.6–0.95. A rate > 1 is a join fan-out bug.
